title: Reading and Use of English – Part 8
description: You are going to read an article about four people who learned the traditional craft of bladesmithing. For questions 47–56, choose from the people A–D. The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Forged in Fire: The Modern Bladesmith

texts.B:
### Marco, The Culinary Specialist
'As a chef, I wanted to understand the soul of my most important tool,' Marco explains. [51]The most daunting aspect was the precise heat treatment; one second too long in the oil and the blade would shatter like glass.[/51] [48]I was surprised to find that my focus in the kitchen improved, as if the discipline of the forge had sharpened my mental clarity.[/48] [53]I learned that a knife is a perfect marriage of utility and art; it must be beautiful but, above all, functional.[/53] [50]I realized that my background in chemistry helped me grasp the molecular changes in the steel during the tempering process.[/50] [47]Initially, the deafening roar of the gas forge and the vibration of the anvil were quite a shock to my system.[/47] It was a fiery revelation.
texts.D:
### Sarah, The Artistic Forger
Sarah creates ornamental blades inspired by mythology. [49]The experience taught me that we have lost a vital connection to the physical objects we use every day in our digital world.[/49] [52]I hadn't anticipated the immense sense of empowerment I’d feel when I first shaped glowing red steel with a hammer.[/52] [47]I was quite startled by how much the soot and the metallic dust affected my breathing in the first month.[/47] [54]What I valued most was the patience of the master smith, who taught me that 'the hammer never lies'.[/54] [56]If I hadn't seen a local artisan's work at a medieval fair, I never would have thought of trying it.[/56] It was a transformative shift.
texts.C:
### Victor, The Tool Restorer
Victor focuses on restoring antique farm tools. 'It’s about preserving heritage,' he notes. [51]I struggled with the scarcity of authentic materials; finding the right high-carbon steel from old leaf springs was a constant challenge.[/51] [48]I noticed that my general physical endurance and core strength reached an all-time high, which was a great side effect.[/48] [53]I realized that these tools were designed to be repaired, not replaced, which is a lesson in sustainability.[/53] [50]I found that the meticulousness I used in my previous career as an aeronautical engineer was perfect for the grinding phase.[/50] [55]It took a while to adjust to the intense heat of the workshop, which I found quite repulsive and exhausting at first.[/55] [52]I’d initially worried I would be too clumsy for such precise work.
texts.A:
### Elena, The Hobbyist Maker
'I wanted a hobby that was completely different from my desk job,' Elena says. [49]The project made me realize that 'mistakes' in the forge are often just opportunities for a new design direction.[/49] [56]I wouldn't have found the studio if my local community college hadn't offered a weekend 'intro to forge' workshop.[/56] [54]I was moved by the camaraderie of the other students, who shared their sharpening stones and encouragement freely.[/54] [47]I hadn't expected the mental fatigue of constant vigilance around 2000-degree metal to be so draining.[/47] [55]I found the initial task of learning the different types of hammers and their specific uses incredibly frustrating.[/55] [52]I’d been concerned that I wouldn't have the upper-body strength to manage the heavier work.

questions.question: mentions being surprised by the physical or sensory impact of the forge environment?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) mentions the roar/vibration, Sarah (B) the soot/dust, and Elena (D) the mental fatigue of vigilance.

questions.question: notes a positive change in their mental clarity or physical endurance as a result of the craft?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) saw his kitchen focus improve, and Victor (C) reached an all-time high in physical endurance.

questions.question: expresses a change in their perspective on our connection to physical objects or the nature of mistakes?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) reflects on our lost connection to objects, and Elena (D) on mistakes as design opportunities.

questions.question: mentions how their professional background or technical skills were advantageous to the process?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) used his chemistry background, and Victor (C) his aeronautical engineering meticulousness.

questions.question: describes a challenge related to technical precision in heat or sourcing specific materials?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) mentions the heat treatment precision, and Victor (C) the scarcity of authentic materials.

questions.question: admits to having had prior doubts or having an unexpected emotional reaction to the work?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) felt empowered, and Victor (C)/Elena (D) worried about clumsiness or strength.

questions.question: reflects on a broader philosophical lesson learned about sustainability or the union of art and utility?
questions.explanation: Marco (A) saw the marriage of art/utility, and Victor (C) learned about sustainability/repair.

questions.question: highlights the value of the patience of a mentor or the camaraderie of fellow learners?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) valued her mentor's patience, and Elena (D) the camaraderie of fellow students.

questions.question: describes a period of frustration or adjustment to the heat or the complexity of tools?
questions.explanation: Victor (C) adjusted to the repulsive heat, and Elena (D) found learning the hammers frustrating.

questions.question: acknowledges the specific external factor or event that led to their involvement?
questions.explanation: Sarah (B) saw an artisan at a fair, and Elena (D) attended a community college workshop.
